base.note: Mgmt FY26 guide midpoint, modest organic growth, MariTide pending

risks.name: Reimbursement & Pricing Pressure
key_drivers: PBM consolidation (94% of Rx in 6 entities)
IRA inflation penalties + Part D redesign

mitigations.action: Diversify payer base away from top 6 PBMs

risks.name: Horizon Integration & Supply-Chain Consolidation
key_drivers: Horizon DIO burden raises group DIO

risks.name: Tax & Regulatory Changes
key_drivers: Puerto Rico Act 60 incentives at risk
Potential $1–3B cash outlay if IRS prevails

scenarios.label: Aggressive lean (−60d)
scenarios.note: Demand-driven replenishment, dual-sourcing, SKU rationalisation and consignment stock pull DIO toward best-in-class. Frees the most cash but raises stock-out risk if a supply disruption hits the leaner buffer.

scenarios.label: Moderate optimise (−30d)
scenarios.note: JIT for high-velocity inputs plus seasonal safety-stock modelling. Balanced: meaningful cash release while keeping resilience against Puerto Rico / single-source shocks.

scenarios.label: Hold (base, 0d)
scenarios.note: Status quo. DIO stays at FY25 levels (~250d) — deep buffer protects against disruption but locks up the most working capital and carries the highest holding cost.

scenarios.label: Build buffer (+30d)
scenarios.note: Pre-emptive safety-stock build ahead of LoE wind-downs, hurricane season, or geopolitical CMO risk. Protects revenue continuity but consumes cash and lifts carrying cost & obsolescence exposure.

drivers: Biologics need 6–9 months from cell culture to release; long cycles force deep in-process and finished-goods stock.
Sterility, potency and stability testing adds weeks; quarantined lots sit as inventory until released.
75% commercial mfg in Puerto Rico and many single-source CMOs force safety-stock buffers against disruption.
Temperature-controlled biologics carry high storage cost and obsolescence/write-off risk near expiry.
Low-volume rare-disease SKUs (TEPEZZA, KRYSTEXXA, UPLIZNA) need disproportionate safety stock.
Pre-LoE builds then post-LoE wind-downs whipsaw inventory and raise markdown risk.
levers: Replace static safety stock with signal-based replenishment
Vendor-managed and pay-on-use stock with top CMOs
Trim long-tail / slow-moving SKUs and pack variants
Dynamic buffer: lean off-season, build pre-hurricane
Systematic wind-down 6–9 mo ahead of patent loss

dio_roadmap.objective: Build the inventory data foundation and stop the bleeding before optimising. You cannot manage what you cannot see at SKU/lot granularity.
steps: Stand up a single inventory data model across Amgen + Horizon ERPs. Tag every SKU/lot with value, age, shelf-life remaining, source site, and demand class (A/B/C). Without this, every later lever is guesswork.

Classify SKUs by value (ABC) and demand variability (XYZ). 'AX' = high-value/stable → run lean; 'CZ' = low-value/erratic → buffer or rationalise. Focus effort on the 20% of SKUs holding ~80% of value.
Flag stock >180 days of supply and within 12 months of expiry. Decide: sell-through, redeploy across geographies, or write-off. Stops aged stock compounding.

Replace one blanket safety-stock policy with per-segment service-level targets (e.g. 99.5% for life-saving oncology, 97% for elective). Over-buffering uniformly is the single biggest source of excess.

dio_roadmap.objective: Replace static, calendar-based safety stock with signal-based replenishment driven by real demand and statistically-sized buffers. This is the largest single lever (~$500–750M).
steps: Size buffers from demand variability + lead-time variability + target service level (not a flat 'X months'). Mathematically right-sizes every buffer; typically releases 15–25% of safety stock.
Monthly integrated business planning: commercial demand signal → supply plan → inventory plan, with one consensus forecast. Cuts the forecast-error buffer that bloats inventory.
Add ML demand sensing on high-velocity products; track MAPE by SKU. Every 5pt accuracy gain lets you safely cut buffer ~3–5%.
Hold semi-finished (bulk drug substance) and finish/label to order where shelf-life allows. Pools variability upstream → less finished-goods safety stock per market.

dio_roadmap.objective: Cut the structural buffers that exist only because supply is concentrated and single-sourced. Lower disruption risk lets you safely hold less.
steps: Qualify a second source for single-source Horizon products (TEPEZZA, KRYSTEXXA). Removes the 'disruption insurance' inventory currently carried against single points of failure.
Move to vendor-managed or pay-on-use inventory for high-volume inputs. Stock sits on supplier's books until consumed — removes it from Amgen DIO.
Consolidate market-level safety stock into regional hubs (risk-pooling). One pooled buffer covers many markets with less total stock.
Bring second-site capacity online to cut Puerto Rico concentration (75%→60–65%). Reduces the geographic-concentration safety stock premium.

dio_roadmap.objective: Lock in the gains so DIO does not drift back. Automate replenishment, govern with hard KPIs, and bake inventory targets into the operating plan and incentives.
steps: Real-time inventory control tower with auto-replenishment within policy bands; exception-based management. Removes manual over-ordering and reaction lag.
Cull long-tail SKUs/pack variants below margin or volume thresholds; gate new-SKU introductions on inventory impact. Fewer SKUs = less aggregate buffer + obsolescence.
Standard 6–9 month run-down ahead of every patent loss (Prolia/XGEVA pattern). Prevents the 30–50 day post-LoE inventory spike and markdown losses.
Make DIO/cash-conversion a board-level KPI in the annual operating plan with targets in supply-chain & commercial scorecards. Sustains behaviour after the program team disbands.
